does: PI-Hunter is an automated red-teaming framework designed to expose and localize prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents, particularly indirect attacks that exploit untrusted external sources. The system constructs realistic, source-aware test cases and iteratively refines them through feedback-driven exploration to trigger agents into retrieving and revealing hidden malicious instructions embedded in external environments. Rather than simply maximizing attack success, PI-Hunter focuses on comprehensive vulnerability exposure and characterization, enabling developers to understand how prompt injections emerge and propagate through agent systems. The framework is evaluated across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, and defense mechanisms, demonstrating substantial improvements in vulnerability detection coverage compared to existing automated red-teaming baselines.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in the security evaluation of agentic LLM systems, extending prior research on prompt injection attacks and red-teaming methodologies. While earlier prompt injection research focused on direct attacks and inference-time defenses, PI-Hunter responds to the emerging threat landscape of agents that interact with untrusted external environments. It builds on automated red-teaming traditions that optimize adversarial examples, but diverges by prioritizing systematic vulnerability discovery rather than attack success rates. The framework connects to work on agent safety, external tool integration vulnerabilities, and systematic security auditing of AI systems. It also relates to research on interpretability and attack surface analysis by focusing on localizing where and how injections occur within agent execution pipelines.
enables: PI-Hunter establishes a foundation for proactive security evaluation of increasingly complex agentic LLM systems. By systematically exposing latent vulnerabilities and their propagation patterns, the work enables developers to design more robust agent architectures and defenses before deployment. The feedback-driven exploration mechanism could inspire future defensive strategies that similarly understand vulnerability emergence rather than simply blocking detected attacks. This framework supports the development of safer agentic AI systems by providing visibility into failure modes, potentially leading to better instruction handling, memory isolation, or source validation techniques. The source-aware test case construction methodology may generalize to auditing other multi-step AI systems that integrate external information, advancing the broader field of systematic AI safety evaluation and vulnerability disclosure practices.
